segments.purgatorio-28-g105: Parrebbe is the present conditional of parere, used impersonally here with maraviglia as the notional subject: “it would seem (a) wonder.” Non negates it. Di là (di + là) is a locative phrase meaning “from/there,” and poi adds the sense “afterwards/any longer.” Word order puts the adverbial di là poi before the noun maraviglia for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-28-g106: Udito is the past participle of udire used adverbially (an absolute participle): “having heard.” Questo is its direct object: “this” (what was just said). The comma marks the participial phrase that modifies the whole main clause.
segments.purgatorio-28-g107: Quando introduces a temporal/hypothetical clause “when/if.” Alcuna is an indefinite adjective (“some/any”) modifying pianta (plant). Sanza is an archaic form of senza (“without”), modifying seme (“seed”); palese qualifies seme as “visible/apparent.” Vi is a locative clitic adverb “there.” The verb is the pronominal appigliarsi (s’appiglia), here present indicative meaning “take hold / root / attach itself,” with the reflexive clitic s’ attached to the verb.
segments.purgatorio-28-g108: Saper is an infinitive here meaning “to know/learn.” Dei is the contracted preposition di + i and functions as a source marker “from” (literally “to learn from”), introducing the reported content introduced by che. La campagna santa is the subject of the following clause; dove tu se’ is a relative/locative clause “where you are” (se’ is an older second-person singular form of essere with the final vowel elided).
segments.purgatorio-28-g109: D’ is the elided form of di and begins the complement of the adjective piena: d’ogne semenza = “of every seed.” Ogne is an archaic form of ogni (“every/each”). È piena is the verbal phrase “is full,” agreeing in gender and number with la campagna.
segments.purgatorio-28-g110: This is a second coordinated clause: frutto ha in sé = “it has fruit within itself,” with in sé (lit. “in itself”) emphasizing that the fruit is contained within the land. Che introduces a relative clause describing that fruit: di là is a locative “there,” non negates the verb, and si schianta is a pronominal/intransitive use of schiantare meaning here “perish/fail” (the reflexive si forming the intransitive sense). Word order places the relative clause after the main clause as usual.
segments.purgatorio-28-g111: L’acqua che vedi is the main subject ('the water that you see'); non surge di vena = 'does not spring from a vein/source'. surge is a present form (poetic spelling of 'sorge'/'surgere') meaning 'rises/springs up'. The relative clause che ristori vapor che gel converta contains two subordinate 'che' clauses; ristori and converta are present-subjunctive/characteristic forms used to describe the hypothetical or defining power of such a vein ('that would restore vapors that turn to frost').
segments.purgatorio-28-g112: come introduces a simile 'like/as'; fiume is the noun being compared. ch’ is a contracted relative 'che' = 'that/which'; acquista e perde are present indicative verbs 'acquires and loses' describing the river's variable 'lena' (an object meaning 'breath/force' here). The structure describes a river that alternately gains and loses strength.
segments.purgatorio-28-g113: ma = 'but/rather' contrasts what follows with the previous simile; esce is present indicative 'issues/flows out' and takes the origin expressed by di fontana ('from a fountain'). salda e certa are postpositive adjectives describing the fountain as 'firm and certain' (adjectives follow the noun in Italian and agree in gender/number with fontana).
segments.purgatorio-28-g114: che begins a relative clause modifying fontana: 'which ...'. tanto introduces the quantity 'so much' and is linked to the comparison in the following line. dal voler di Dio is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the will of God' (dal = da + il). riprende is present indicative of riprendere, here meaning 'takes back' or 'reclaims' — the fountain's output is governed by God's will.
segments.purgatorio-28-g115: quant’ is contracted quanto = 'as much as'; ella is the feminine pronoun 'it/she' referring back to la fontana; versa is present indicative 'pours/flows out'; da due parti aperta is an ablative phrase 'being open on two sides' (da indicates source or manner here, and aperta is an adjective agreeing with fontana/ella). The whole phrase sets up a balance: the fountain's intake from God's will equals what it pours out through two openings.
segments.purgatorio-28-g116: Da questa parte is an adverbial phrase 'from this side'; con virtù discende is 'descends with power/virtue' (discende is present indicative, third person singular). che introduces a relative clause ('that') and toglie is present indicative of togliere 'takes away' with altrui ('from others') as the indirect object; memoria del peccato is the direct object 'the memory of sin'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g117: da l’altra = 'from the other [side]'; d’ogne ben fatto is a genitive-of phrase 'of every well-done (deed)' where ogne = every and ben fatto = 'goodly/virtuous deed' (ben as noun/adverb combined with past participle fatto). la is a feminine singular pronoun referring back (contextually to 'memory' or 'that [thing]') and rende is present indicative of rendere 'gives back/returns' — so the clause means the opposite action happens from the other side: it restores it to every good act.
segments.purgatorio-28-g53: Tosto che is a temporal conjunction meaning 'as soon as' and introduces a subordinate clause. Fu is the passato remoto (third singular) of essere meaning 'was' or 'came to be' located; là and dove set the location ('there' / 'where'), and the relative clause 'dove l'erbe sono bagnate già da l'onde del bel fiume' uses the present indicative sono + past participle bagnate to describe the grasses' state ('are wet'); da l'onde means 'by/from the waves' and bel is the usual aphetic form of bello used before masculine nouns.
segments.purgatorio-28-g54: Di introduces the noun phrase that names the content of the gift (dono di + infinitive). Levar is the infinitive 'to raise' (here 'to lift'), li occhi suoi is the direct object with the masculine plural article li and the possessive suoi after the noun, mi is the indirect object 'to me', fece is passato remoto of fare 'made', and dono is 'gift' — the whole idiom mi fece dono di + infinitive = 'she gave me the gift of ...'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g55: Non credo che introduces a negative statement of belief and (in classical/poetic Italian) is followed by the imperfect subjunctive splendesse to express doubt about a past or hypothetical event. Tanto lume is a degree expression 'so much / such a light' and sotto le ciglia a Venere places that light physically 'under Venus's eyelashes' — a is the preposition marking the person affected ('at/on/to Venus').
segments.purgatorio-28-g56: Trafitta is a past participle ('pierced') agreeing with the feminine noun it modifies (Venere). Dal is the contraction of da + il marking the agent ('by the son'), figlio is 'son' (here Cupid), and the phrase fuor di tutto suo costume is an archaic way of saying 'outside / contrary to all her custom' (fuor di = fuori da = outside of).
segments.purgatorio-28-g57: Ella is the subject 'she'; ridea is the imperfect indicative of ridere (she was laughing or laughed repeatedly) describing a continuous or habitual past action. Da l'altra riva dritta places her position 'from the opposite bank' with dritta functioning adverbially to mean 'directly' or 'straight on.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g58: Trattando is a gerund ('handling, moving, gesturing') indicating a concurrent action ('while she laughed'). Più here is comparative 'more' and color refers to 'colours' or visual displays; con le sue mani specifies the means 'with her hands.' The whole phrase describes her gesturing to show a greater variety or intensity of colours.
segments.purgatorio-28-g59: Che here functions as a comparative 'than' linking the gesture to what follows. L'alta terra is 'the high land' or high bank, sanza is the archaic form of senza 'without', seme is 'seed', and gitta (present indicative) means 'throws' or 'casts' — a poetic present describing the land 'casting up' things without seed, used for contrast with the woman's more vivid display.
segments.purgatorio-28-g60: facea is the imperfect (poetic form facea for modern faceva) of fare, here meaning 'made' or 'caused'. Tre passi is a measure of distance placed before the verb as the direct object ('three steps'); ci = 'us' is the personal pronoun receiving that action; il fiume is the grammatical subject placed after the verb. lontani is an adjective agreeing with ci (us), meaning 'distant'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g61: ma is the adversative 'but' introducing the contrast; Elesponto is the proper name (Hellespont) and functions as the new subject of the following clause.
segments.purgatorio-28-g62: là ’ve is a poetic contraction of 'là ove' = 'there where'; passò is passato remoto (third person singular) of passare, 'crossed', and Serse is the postverbal subject (inverted order: verb + subject is common in poetry).
segments.purgatorio-28-g63: ancora is an adverb 'still/yet'; freno is a noun used predicatively ('a restraint/brake') and takes the dative/indirect complement introduced by a; tutti orgogli umani ('all human prides') is the group to which the restraint applies. Note the copula 'is' is omitted (elliptical), a common poetic compression.
segments.purgatorio-28-g64: più odio ... non sofferse is a comparative/negative construction: 'did not suffer more hatred' (i.e. 'did not endure any greater hatred'). da + personal name (da Leandro) marks the agent in a passive or adverse sense ('from Leander' / 'by Leander'). per + verb (mareggiare) gives the reason 'because of the sea-storming'; intra = 'between'. sofferse is passato remoto of soffrire, here 'suffered/endured'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g65: che introduces the comparison clause ('than'); quel = 'that one' (a demonstrative pronoun referring back to the other river/thing); da me = 'by me' (agent/experiencer marked with da); perch' is poetic contraction of perché = 'because'; allor = 'then'; s’aperse is reflexive passato remoto (si aperse) 'opened itself' or 'did not open'—the reflexive pronoun s' is attached before the verb in the poetic form.
segments.purgatorio-28-g131: Io mi rivolsi: 'rivolsi' is passato remoto, 1st person singular, meaning 'I turned' or 'I turned myself back'; the clitic 'mi' is reflexive ('myself'). '’n dietro' is a contracted form of 'in dietro' meaning 'backwards' or 'behind'. 'tutto' here is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'completely' or 'altogether'. The preposition 'a' + 'miei poeti' gives the direction/target of the turning ('to my poets').
segments.purgatorio-28-g132: 'e vidi' is another passato remoto, 'and I saw'; the conjunction 'che' introduces the subordinate clause ('that ...'). 'con riso' is an adverbial phrase of manner ('with a smile' / 'with laughter'). Note the inverted order in the subordinate clause: the past participle 'udito' (from 'udire', 'heard') appears before the auxiliary 'avëan' (archaic spelling of 'avevano', imperfect auxiliary 'they had'), so 'udito avëan' = 'they had heard'. 'l’ultimo costrutto' is the direct object ('the last construction/phrase/utterance') — a poetic way to refer to the final words or composition.
segments.purgatorio-28-g133: 'poi' = 'then'. 'a la bella donna' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the beautiful lady' (Beatrice), with 'la' as the article. 'torna’' is the poet's orthography for passato remoto 3rd singular 'tornò' ('turned'); the grammatical subject is 'il viso' ('the face'), so the literal structure is 'the face turned to the lady' — i.e. the narrator turned his face toward her (Dante often uses a body part as the subject of such verbs).
segments.purgatorio-28-g29: Coi piè ristetti e con li occhi passai di là dal fiumicello: 'Coi' = contracted preposition + article meaning 'with the'. 'piè' (foot) is plural in sense but here 'with my feet' (instrumental). 'ristetti' is a past-tense verb meaning 'I stopped/paused' (archaic formation); its precise nuance is 'I stood still'. 'e con li occhi passai' uses the same instrument idea with 'con' + plural article 'li' and 'occhi' (gaze/eyes): 'I cast my eyes' or 'I passed my sight'—'passai' is simple past 'I passed' and governs the following 'di là dal fiumicello' (from there by the little stream), where 'di là' = 'from there' and 'dal fiumicello' = 'by/from the little river'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g30: per mirare la gran varïazion d’i freschi mai: 'per' introduces purpose 'in order to/for', so 'per mirare' = 'to look at/admire'. 'la gran varïazion' = 'the great variation/variety'. 'd’i' is the contraction 'di' + article 'i' = 'of the'; 'freschi mai' is a poetic expression meaning 'fresh Mays' (springtime blossoms) — grammatically 'mai' here is a substantive/poetic plural but is treated as the object of 'varïazion'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g31: e là m’apparve: simple clause 'and there appeared to me'. 'm’' is the clitic for 'mi' (to me), attached to the verb 'apparve' (past historic 'appeared'). Word order places the adverb 'là' before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-28-g32: sì com’ elli appare subitamente cosa che disvia per maraviglia tutto altro pensare: 'sì com’' = 'just as/as'. 'elli' here is a poetic/archaic form (not the modern pronoun) used in the text and must be read in context (the syntax treats it as a point of comparison); 'appare' is present infinitive-type sense 'appears/shows'. 'subitamente' = 'suddenly'. The relative clause 'cosa che disvia per maraviglia tutto altro pensare' means 'a thing that diverts, through wonder, every other thought' — 'disvia' (turns aside/diverts) has active sense and the infinitive/noun 'pensare' is used as 'thought'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g33: una donna soletta che si gia e cantando e scegliendo fior da fiore ond’ era pinta tutta la sua via: 'una donna soletta' = 'a solitary woman'. 'che si gia' — 'si' is the reflexive particle and 'gia' (here a verb form, archaic) means 'was moving/going already' (i.e. she was proceeding). 'e cantando e scegliendo fior da fiore' = 'and singing and picking/choosing flower by flower'. 'ond’ era pinta tutta la sua via' = 'from which her whole path/way was painted' — 'ond’' = 'whence/from which', 'era pinta' = 'was painted' (passive past), 'tutta la sua via' = 'all along her path'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g34: «Deh,: an interjection of appeal—'Deh' expresses entreaty or pleading ('Ah'/'Please'). In Dante it opens a polite/humble address.
segments.purgatorio-28-g35: bella donna,: direct address 'beautiful/ fair lady'. 'bella' = adjective before noun (standard epithet), 'donna' = lady/woman.
segments.purgatorio-28-g36: che a’ raggi d’amore ti scaldi,: 'che' introduces a relative clause describing the lady. 'a’ raggi d’amore' = 'by/to the rays of love' (metaphorical); 'ti scaldi' is present subjunctive/indicative form of 'scaldare' used impersonally/reflexively here meaning 'you are warmed' or 'you are warmed for yourself'—literal 'you warm yourself' but idiomatically 'you are warmed'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g37: s’i’ vo’ credere a’ sembianti che soglion esser testimon del core,: 's’ i’ vo’' is an archaic sequence meaning 'if I am to believe' (literally 'if I go to believe'); 'credere a’ sembianti' = 'believe the appearances/looks'. 'che soglion esser testimon del core' = 'which are accustomed to be witnesses of the heart' — 'soglion' = 'are wont' (habitual present), 'esser' = infinitive 'to be', 'testimon del core' = 'testimony/witness of the heart'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g38: vegnati in voglia di trarreti avanti»,: 'vegnati' is an imperative/optative form ('deign/come' or 'be moved to'), followed by 'in voglia' = 'into a will/desire' (i.e. 'be willing'), 'di trarreti avanti' = 'to draw/bring yourself forward' (middle reflexive use of 'trarre' with clitic 'ti' attached to the infinitive; 'avanti' = 'forward'). The whole is a polite request to move closer.
segments.purgatorio-28-g39: diss’ io a lei,: 'diss’' = contracted 'dissi' (I said) or poetic 'diss’', followed by subject 'io' (emphatic 'I') and indirect object 'a lei' = 'to her'. This marks the speaker tagging his speech.
segments.purgatorio-28-g40: «verso questa rivera,: 'verso' = 'toward', 'questa' = demonstrative 'this', 'rivera' = 'bank/shore' — a simple direction/location phrase beginning the quoted imperative.
segments.purgatorio-28-g41: tanto ch’io possa intender che tu canti.: 'tanto ch’io possa intender' = 'so that I may be able to perceive/hear/understand' — 'possa' is subjunctive expressing potentiality/purpose; 'intender' here can mean 'to hear/understand (what is sung)'. 'che tu canti' is a subordinate clause 'that you sing' (present subjunctive).
segments.purgatorio-28-g42: Tu mi fai rimembrar dove e qual era Proserpina nel tempo che perdette la madre lei,: 'Tu mi fai rimembrar' = 'You cause me to remember' or 'You remind me' ('fai' + infinitive 'rimembrar' = causative 'make/bring to mind'). 'dove e qual era Proserpina' = 'where and what sort she was (the nature/role of Proserpina)'. 'nel tempo che perdette la madre lei' = 'at the time when she lost her mother' — 'perdette' is past historic 'lost'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g43: ed ella primavera».: closing tag 'and she was Spring' — 'primavera' functions as a predicative noun: Proserpina is identified with the season of Spring.
segments.purgatorio-28-g44: Come si volge,: 'Come' = 'as/like', 'si volge' = reflexive verb 'turns herself/turns' — a simile-comparison opening ('As she turns...').
segments.purgatorio-28-g45: con le piante strette a terra e intra sé,: 'con le piante' = 'with the soles' (piante = soles of the feet), 'strette a terra' = 'pressed/tight against the ground', 'e intra sé' = 'and inward to herself' (a gesture of modesty/self-containment). Adjective agreement: 'strette' agrees with 'piante'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g46: donna che balli,: 'like a woman who dances' — relative 'che' + present subjunctive/optative 'balli' gives vivid, generalizing description of the movement.
segments.purgatorio-28-g47: e piede innanzi piede a pena mette,: 'e' = 'and'; the phrase 'piede innanzi piede' = 'foot before foot' (step by step); 'a pena mette' = 'scarcely puts' or 'with difficulty sets' — 'a pena' = 'hardly/scarcely', 'mette' = 'puts/sets'. The clause conveys a careful, timid stepping like a dance.
segments.purgatorio-28-g48: volsesi in su i vermigli e in su i gialli fioretti verso me,: 'volsesi' (poetic form) = 'she turns'/'she directs', 'in su i vermigli e in su i gialli fioretti' = 'upward the little vermilion and little yellow flowers' (the flowers are lifted/turned up), 'verso me' = 'toward me'. 'in su' emphasizes upward motion.
segments.purgatorio-28-g49: non altrimenti che vergine che li occhi onesti avvalli;: 'non altrimenti che' = 'no otherwise than' (i.e. 'just as'), introducing the comparison. 'vergine che li occhi onesti avvalli' = 'a maiden who lowers/shields her modest eyes' — 'onesti' = 'modest/chaste', 'avvalli' is a poetic verb meaning 'to lower/avert' here (the dictionary head may not list this exact sense but context makes it 'to lower/avert one's gaze').
segments.purgatorio-28-g50: e fece i prieghi miei esser contenti,: 'e fece' = 'and she made', 'i prieghi miei' = 'my entreaties/prayers', 'esser contenti' = 'be satisfied/fulfilled' — causative 'fece' + infinitive 'esser' indicates she brought about the fulfilment of his petitions.
segments.purgatorio-28-g51: sì appressando sé,: 'sì' = 'thus/so', 'appressando sé' = 'approaching herself' (present participle, reflexive) — she draws near, the manner clause for the previous result.
segments.purgatorio-28-g52: che ’l dolce suono veniva a me co’ suoi intendimenti.: 'che' introduces result/explanatory clause: 'that the sweet sound came to me with her intentions' — 'veniva' (imperfect) = 'came' or 'was coming', 'a me' = 'to me', 'co’' = contracted 'con i' = 'with the', 'suoi intendimenti' = 'her intentions/meanings' (i.e. the purpose/feeling behind the sound accompanied the sound itself).
segments.purgatorio-28-g118: "Quinci" is an adverb meaning 'from here' or 'on this side' placed before the toponym; "Letè" (archaic spelling) is the proper name Lethe. The short clause simply locates the river/stream by place.
segments.purgatorio-28-g119: "così" here links by resemblance ('thus' / 'in the same way'); "da l’altro lato" is a prepositional phrase ('from/on the other side') with the elided article "l’" before "altro lato"; "Eünoè si chiama" is an impersonal/passive construction meaning 'is called Eunoe' ("si" marks the passive/impersonal, "chiama" = 'call/name').
segments.purgatorio-28-g120: "e non adopra" is an impersonal negative statement ('and it is not used/it is of no use'); the subordinate introduced by "se" functions as concessive/conditional 'unless' and places the requirement that follows. "quinci e quindi" are paired adverbs 'from here and from there', "pria" ('first/before') precedes "non è gustato" ('is not tasted') where "è + past participle" yields a passive/periphrastic sense 'has been tasted / is tasted'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g121: "a tutti altri sapori" is a comparative frame ('with respect to / compared to all other flavours'); the archaic demonstrative "esto" ('this one/this here') is placed before the verb for emphasis, and "è di sopra" literally 'is above' (= 'surpasses'). The whole line means 'this is superior to all other tastes.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g122: "E avvegna ch’" is a concessive formula meaning 'and although' ("avvegna che" triggers the subjunctive). "assai possa esser sazia la sete tua" uses the present subjunctive "possa essere" ('may be / can be') with the predicate adjective "sazia" ('satiated') and the noun phrase "la sete tua" ('your thirst') as the grammatical subject. "perch’ io più non ti scuopra" gives the reason/cause ('because I no longer reveal/uncover you'); note the archaic/poetic form "scuopra" (1st person) used here.
segments.purgatorio-28-g123: "darotti" is an archaic contracted future form meaning 'I will give you' (darò + ti fused); it carries a clitic object. "un corollario ancor" means 'one more corollary' or 'an additional corollary' and "per grazia" 'as a favour' or 'by grace'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g124: "né credo che" = 'nor do I believe that'; it introduces a subordinate clause with the subjunctive "sia". "’l mio dir" treats the infinitive/noun "dir" ('saying/telling') as a substantive ('my telling/words'), and "ti sia men caro" means 'is less dear to you' ("men" = less, "caro" = dear/pleasant).
segments.purgatorio-28-g125: Here "se" functions as a conditional/'if' (the provided sense list is incorrect for this use). "oltre promession teco" = 'beyond a promise with you' or 'besides mere promise, in your company', and "si spazia" is a reflexive/impersonal present ('it ranges / it extends itself') meaning 'if my speech ranges beyond mere promise'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g126: "Quelli" is a plural demonstrative pronoun ('those') and is followed by the relative "ch’" ('who/that'). "anticamente" ('in ancient times') modifies the verb; "poetaro" here is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'composed poetically / sang' (the provided sense list treats it as a noun, so the verb sense is best read as an archaic past-tense verb). The phrase "l’età de l’oro e suo stato felice" is the object: 'the Age of Gold and its happy state.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g127: "forse" ('perhaps') qualifies the whole clause. "in Parnaso" names the poetic mountain (Parnassus) as the location; "esto loco" ('this place', archaic demonstrative "esto") is the object of the verb; "sognaro" is an archaic past-tense verb form meaning 'they dreamed' or 'they imagined' (the provided senses list treats it as a noun, but context makes it a verb here).
segments.purgatorio-28-g128: "Qui" ('here') is fronted for emphasis and "fu" is the simple past of "essere" ('was'). The predicate adjective "innocente" follows the verb while the noun phrase "l’umana radice" ('the human root' = human race/origin) is placed after the verb in inverted order for poetical emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-28-g129: This line is intentionally elliptical: "qui primavera sempre e ogne frutto" omits the verb (understood from the preceding "fu"). It lists attributes present in that place: 'here springtime always and every fruit' (parallel structure).
segments.purgatorio-28-g130: The inversion "nettare è questo" places the noun "nettare" ('nectar') first for emphasis, with the copula "è" ('is') and demonstrative "questo" ('this'). "di che" = 'of which' (relative use) and "ciascun dice" ('each/everyone says') closes the relative clause: 'this is the nectar of which everyone speaks.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g66: “Voi siete nuovi” is a simple copular clause: Voi is the 2nd-person plural subject pronoun; siete is the present indicative (2nd pl.) of essere; nuovi is a predicate adjective in the plural (masculine) agreeing with voi — literally “You are new/newcomers.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g67: e forse perch’ io rido is a parenthetical causal comment: e forse (“and perhaps”) introduces a conjecture; perch’ is the contracted perché meaning “because”; io rido is present tense 1st-person (I laugh) explaining the possible reason for their unease.
segments.purgatorio-28-g68: cominciò ella is the narrative tag: cominciò is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of cominciare meaning “(she) began,” and ella is the subject pronoun “she” (here used post-verb for poetic word order).
segments.purgatorio-28-g69: “in questo luogo eletto a l’umana natura per suo nido” is a prepositional phrase with an adjectival past participle: in questo luogo = “in this place”; eletto is past participle used adjectivally (“chosen”); a l’umana natura is a dative-like phrase meaning “for human nature”; per suo nido indicates purpose/role (“as its nest” or “for its nest”).
segments.purgatorio-28-g70: maravigliando is a present participle/gerund conveying cause or attendant circumstance (“marvelling”); tienvi is tiene + clitic vi and functions as a 3rd-person singular verb meaning “keeps in you / holds (to you)” (so: wonder keeps in you); alcun sospetto = “some suspicion.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g71: ma luce rende il salmo Delectasti has inverted word order: il salmo (the Psalm) is the logical subject, rende (3rd sg. present of rendere) is the verb “gives / renders,” and luce is the direct object placed before the verb for emphasis — “but the Psalm Delectasti gives light.” Delectasti is the Latin title quoted as a proper name.
segments.purgatorio-28-g72: che introduces a relative clause modifying luce; puote is an archaic present of potere (3rd sg.) meaning “can” or “is able”; disnebbiar is the infinitive “to clear the fog/dispell the cloud”; vostro intelletto is the direct object “your intellect/understanding.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g73: E tu che se’ dinanzi e mi pregasti is a complex address: E tu (“and you”) followed by a relative clause che se’ dinanzi (“who are in front/ahead”); e mi pregasti is a coordinated clause “and asked me” (pregasti = passato remoto of pregare = you requested/asked me).
segments.purgatorio-28-g74: dì is the imperative of dire (“say/tell”); s’ here stands for se (“if/whether”) introducing an indirect interrogative; altro vuoli udir = “you wish to hear anything else” with vuoli an archaic 2nd-person present meaning “you want/wish,” and udir the infinitive “to hear.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g75: ch’i’ venni presta ... tanto che basti is a final/explanatory clause: ch’ = che (“that”); i (here representing io in contracted form) with venni (passato remoto of venire, “I came”) expresses “I have come” or “I am come”; presta is an adjective “ready”; ad ogne tua question = “for every question of yours”; tanto che basti uses tanto che + subjunctive basti (3rd sg.) meaning “so far as will be sufficient.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g76: “L’acqua” is simply the noun phrase the speaker utters as the topic or reason: L’ is the elided article and acqua is the noun “water.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g77: diss’ io is the narrative tag “I said”: diss’ is an archaic/poetic form of dissi/dissi' (passato remoto of dire) with the clitic apostrophe; io is the subject pronoun “I.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g78: This longer clause coordinates the two percepts as causes of a new belief: e = “and”; ’l suon de la foresta = “the sound/noise of the wood”; impugnan is 3rd-pl. present of impugnare meaning “they seize/press upon” (here: they affect me internally); dentro a me = “within me”; novella fede = “a new belief/report” (novella functioning as “new”/“news”); di cosa = “about something”; ch’io udi’ = relative clause “that I heard” (udi’ = passato remoto of udire, 1st person); contraria a questa = “contrary to this.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g79: Ond’ (onde/ond) is a connective meaning “therefore/whence,” used here to introduce her reply; ella is the subject pronoun “she.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g80: Io dicerò come procede per sua cagion ciò ch’ammirar ti face is a promise to explain: Io dicerò = future first-person “I will say/tell”; come procede = “how it proceeds / how it comes about”; per sua cagion = “by its own cause”; ciò = “that which / this thing”; ch’ammirar ti face = a relative clause in which ammirar is an infinitive “to marvel” and face is an older 3rd-person form of fare meaning “makes/causes” — together “that which causes you to marvel.”
segments.purgatorio-28-g81: e purgherò la nebbia che ti fiede is a closing promise: e purgherò = “and I will purge/cleanse” (future 1st sg.); la nebbia = “the fog/haze” (figurative obscurity); che ti fiede is a relative clause where fiede (3rd sg. present of fiedere) means “strikes / smites” (figuratively: that strikes your mind).
segments.purgatorio-28-g1: This is a noun phrase headed by the adjective 'Vago' (here an adjective meaning 'desirous' or 'eager') followed by the infinitival complement 'di cercar' introduced by the preposition 'di'. 'dentro e dintorno' are adverbial locatives ('inside and around') placed after the verb phrase they govern; the adjectives 'spessa e viva' follow the feminine noun 'foresta' and agree with it.
segments.purgatorio-28-g2: The relative clause begins with 'ch’' (= che, 'that') and has the indirect locative 'a li occhi' = 'to/for the eyes' (meaning 'in the sight of') followed by the imperfect 'temperava' (3sg imperfect indicative) meaning 'softened/tempered'. 'il novo giorno' is the subject of that verb ('the new day').
segments.purgatorio-28-g3: 'sanza' is an archaic form of 'senza' ('without'); here it takes the comparative marker 'più' to mean 'no longer' or 'any further', and it governs the infinitive 'aspettar' (infinitive without the modern final -e), so the phrase means 'without waiting any longer.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g4: 'lasciai' is the passato remoto (1st person singular) of 'lasciare' meaning 'I left' or 'I abandoned'; the direct object 'la riva' ('the bank/shore') follows in normal order.
segments.purgatorio-28-g5: 'prendendo' is a present gerund ('taking' / 'having taken') that sets up a circumstance for the main clause; 'la campagna' (the countryside) is its object, and the adverbial repetition 'lento lento' intensifies the slowness. 'su per lo suol' is a locative phrase meaning 'up over the ground/soil' (note: the provided senses list does not include the ordinary meaning 'ground' for 'suol', so that item is marked as unmatched). The relative clause 'che d’ogne parte auliva' modifies 'suol' and says that it is 'from every side full of olive (trees/groves).'
segments.purgatorio-28-g6: A short noun phrase: the indefinite article with elision 'Un’' before a vowel, the feminine noun 'aura' and the adjective 'dolce' following the noun (common word order in Italian poetry).
segments.purgatorio-28-g7: An infinitival clause introduced by the archaic preposition 'sanza' ('without'): 'sanza mutamento avere in sé' = 'without having any change in itself'. 'avere in sé' is an idiomatic infinitive phrase meaning 'to contain/have within itself.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g8: 'mi' is the clitic object pronoun 'to me'; 'feria' is an imperfect form (3sg) of the archaic verb 'ferire/ferire' used here in the sense 'to strike' or 'to touch' ('struck my brow'). 'per la fronte' is an oblique phrase 'on/over the forehead'. The comparative phrase 'non di più colpo che soave vento' literally 'not with more a blow than a gentle wind' uses 'non... che' to set up the comparison (i.e. it struck no more harshly than a gentle wind).
segments.purgatorio-28-g9: 'per cui' = 'by which/through which' introduces the following clause; 'le fronde' is the (plural) subject 'the fronds/leaves'. Note that the provided sense list for 'fronde' contains verb senses rather than the noun 'leaves', so the noun sense is not matched in the supplied list.
segments.purgatorio-28-g10: 'tremolando' is a present participle/gerund functioning adverbially: 'trembling' (the leaves trembling). It modifies the verb of the clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-28-g11: 'pronte' (feminine plural) agrees with 'le fronde' and means 'ready'; 'tutte quante' is an emphatic plural expression meaning 'all of them' (here 'quante' has the sense 'all that' / 'the whole lot'). 'piegavano' is imperfect indicative (3pl) 'were bending/ inclined' and the prepositional phrase 'a la parte u’ la prim’ ombra gitta il santo monte' indicates the direction: 'toward the place where the first shade casts the holy mountain.' 'u’' = 'ove' (where), and 'prim’' is contracted 'prima.' 'gitta' (from 'gittare') means 'casts'/'throws out'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g12: 'non però' = 'not, however' (negation with a concessive nuance). 'dal loro esser dritto sparte tanto' is a complex phrase: 'dal' (from the) + 'loro' (their) + infinitive-noun 'esser dritto' ('being upright') + past participle/adjective 'sparte' ('scattered/separated'), with 'tanto' ('so much') limiting the degree: 'not, however, so much separated from being upright.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g13: The result clause introduced by 'che' ('that') uses the (archaic) subjunctive/conditional form 'lasciasser' meaning 'would cease/leave off' or 'would stop'. 'li augelletti' (the little birds) are the subject; 'per le cime' ('on the treetops') indicates position; 'd’operare ogne lor arte' = 'from carrying out all their craft/skill' (i.e. stop singing/working their arts).
segments.purgatorio-28-g14: 'ma' = 'but'; 'con piena letizia' = 'with full joy'; 'l’ore prime' the article contraction and plural noun 'ore' (hours) with 'prime' (first/early) — in poetry 'l’ore' marks the hours as a collective time period. This phrase sets up the subject/time when the next verb acts.
segments.purgatorio-28-g15: 'cantando' is another gerund/participle ('singing') and is attached to the temporal phrase 'l’ore prime', describing the activity performed at that time (the singers sing as the hours begin).
segments.purgatorio-28-g16: 'ricevieno' is an archaic plural present form (cf. modern 'ricevono') meaning 'they receive' or 'they take in'; 'intra le foglie' = 'within the leaves' (intra = within). The clause says that the first hours, singing, receive/are received inside the leaves.
segments.purgatorio-28-g17: Relative clause introduced by 'che' ('which'): 'tenevan' is imperfect indicative (3pl) 'they kept/held'; 'bordone' here is used in the musical/metaphorical sense 'drone/accompaniment' (i.e. they kept time or accompaniment) and 'a le sue rime' means 'to/with his verses' — 'sue' refers back to the source of the verses. The clause says the leaves served as accompaniment to those rhymes.
segments.purgatorio-28-g18: 'tal qual... qual' is a simile marker 'such as' / 'just as'. 'di ramo in ramo' 'from branch to branch' is a repeated prepositional phrase indicating movement; 'si raccoglie' is a reflexive present 'it gathers itself' or 'it gathers' (impersonal/reciprocal sense), and the prepositional phrase 'per la pineta in su ’l lito di Chiassi' indicates the path 'through the pinewood up onto the strand/coast called Chiassi' (Chiassi is a personified noun meaning noise/uproar here).
segments.purgatorio-28-g19: 'quand’' = 'quando' ('when'). 'Ëolo' is the proper name 'Aeolus'. 'scilocco' is an epithet (here pejorative: 'foolish' / 'silly'); 'fuor' is an adverb ('out') and 'discioglie' is present indicative (3sg) 'loosens'/'sets loose' — together 'when Æolus, foolish, lets [the winds] out.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g20: 'Già' = 'already/by now'. 'm’avean trasportato' is a compound past (imperfect auxiliary 'avean' + past participle 'trasportato') meaning 'had carried me' — poetic / archaic ordering where 'i lenti passi' ('the slow steps') is the grammatical subject personified. 'dentro a la selva antica' is the goal phrase and 'tanto' ('so much') quantifies the extent.
segments.purgatorio-28-g21: Result clause introduced by 'ch’' ('that'): 'io non potea rivedere' = 'I could not see/recognize again' with 'potea' the imperfect of 'potere'. 'ond’' = 'whence' or 'from which' and 'io mi ’ntrassi' is an archaic/contracted form of 'intrassi' (entered) referring back to the place of entry; the clause means 'so that I could not see whence I had entered.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g22: 'ed ecco' = 'and behold'; the phrase 'più andar mi tolse un rio' literally places 'più andar' (more going/further walking) before the verb 'tolse' (past 'took away'/'took from me') with 'un rio' the subject causing the action — a compact, poetic word order meaning roughly 'and behold, a stream carried me on' or 'a stream made me proceed further' (Tosc. 'tolse' = 'took' with the clitic 'mi').
segments.purgatorio-28-g23: Relative clause 'che' = 'which': '’nver’' (contraction of 'in ver' = 'toward') + 'sinistra' = 'to the left'; 'con sue picciole onde' = 'with its small waves'; 'piegava' (3sg imperfect) 'bent' and the object is 'l’erba' 'the grass' followed by a relative 'che ’n sua ripa uscìo' meaning 'that rose/issued on its bank' (archaic 'uscìo' = 'emerged/rose up'). The pronoun 'n' representing a contraction is irregular in the senses list and is therefore unmatched.
segments.purgatorio-28-g24: 'Tutte l’acque' = 'all the waters'; 'che son di qua' = 'that are on this side'; 'più monde' = 'more purified / purer' (comparative, with 'più' + past participle/adjective 'monde' from 'mondare'). Word order is regular: noun phrase + relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-28-g25: 'parrieno' is an archaic verbal form meaning 'seemed' (modern 'parevano'), but the provided sense list did not supply this meaning and is therefore unmatched. The rest is straightforward: 'avere in sé mistura alcuna' = 'to have in itself some mixture' and 'verso di quella' = 'toward/of that one' (i.e. a mixing toward that other water).
segments.purgatorio-28-g26: A short relative clause: 'che' = 'which'; 'nulla' = 'nothing'; 'nasconde' (3sg present) = 'hides/conceals'. The clause refers back to 'quella' (that one) and asserts it 'hides nothing.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g27: 'avvegna che' is a concessive conjunction 'although' or 'even though'; the clause uses a subjunctive 'si mova' (3sg subjunctive of 'muovere' here 'to move') because of the concessive construction. 'bruna bruna' is a duplicated adjective for emphasis ('dark brown, dark brown'), and 'sotto l’ombra perpetüa' is a locative phrase 'under the perpetual shade.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g28: Relative clause with 'che' = 'which': 'mai' together with the negation 'non' yields the sense 'never' here; 'raggiar' (infinitive) means 'to radiate/shine', and 'non lascia' (3sg present) means 'does not allow'. The objects 'sole' and 'luna' here mean 'sun' and 'moon' respectively, but the provided senses list for 'sole' did not include 'sun' and is therefore marked as unmatched. 'ivi' = 'there', and 'né' = 'nor'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g82: Lo + sommo + Ben: the article lo marks a definite, emphatic nominal phrase; sommo is an adjective 'supreme/highest' modifying Ben, here used as a substantive ('the Good') and personified. The whole phrase names God as the supreme Good.
segments.purgatorio-28-g83: che = relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing a clause about 'the Highest Good'. solo = 'only' limiting the following element; esso is the pronoun referring back to 'Ben' (emphatic 'it/he'); a sé piace is a reflexive construction with piacere: 'piace' (3rd sing. present) meaning 'finds pleasure/delights' and a sé 'to himself'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g84: fé is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of fare, 'made/caused'. l’uom (elided article + uom) = 'the man' (archaic form of uomo). buono is a predicative adjective 'good'. e a bene: the conjunction e 'and' connects, while a bene ('to/into good') expresses orientation/tendency toward goodness.
segments.purgatorio-28-g85: e questo loco = 'and this place' (subject/object phrase). diede is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of dare 'gave'. per introduces the purpose/role ('as' / 'for the purpose of'); arr’ is a clipped, poetic form (written with apostrophe) whose sense here is archaic/poetical and best understood as 'a beginning/step/approach' toward something; a lui = indirect object 'to him'; d’ etterna pace = 'of/for eternal peace' (d' = di). The whole means 'gave this place to him as a step toward eternal peace.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g86: Per sua difalta = 'because of his fault' or 'through his lack/shortcoming' (per + possessive + difalta, an archaic noun). qui 'here' indicates location. dimorò is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of dimorare 'stayed/dwelt'. poco = 'a little/for a short time'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g87: per sua difalta repeats 'because of his fault'. in pianto e in affanno sets the result-state with in + noun: 'into weeping and into distress/anguish'. cambiò is passato remoto of cambiare 'changed/turned (something into something)'; here the verb takes onesto riso e dolce gioco as its direct object, meaning 'he turned honest laughter and sweet play into weeping and anguish.' Note the inversion: the result (in pianto...) is placed before the verb.
segments.purgatorio-28-g88: Perché = 'because'. ’l turbar uses an infinitive as a noun ('the disturbance/trouble'); che introduces a relative clause describing that disturbance. sotto da sé = 'beneath (them), from themselves' (literally 'under from itself/themselves'), i.e. beneath the elements; fanno is present (3rd pl.) of fare 'make/do' agreeing with the understood plural notion of the exhalations. l’essalazion de l’acqua e de la terra = 'the exhalation(s) of water and of earth' (de = archaic di).
segments.purgatorio-28-g89: che = 'which' (relative). quanto posson = 'as much as they can' (quanto = 'as much as'); dietro al calor vanno = 'they go after/follow the heat' (dietro = 'after/behind', al = a + il). vanno is present of andare 'go' used here as 'follow/seek'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g90: a l’uomo = literally 'to/against man' (prepositional phrase indicating the target of hostility). non facesse is imperfect subjunctive (facesse) with negation; here it expresses the desired/resulting prevention ('so that it would not make/should not make'). alcuna guerra = 'any war/strife' (alcuna = any/some, guerra = war/attack).
segments.purgatorio-28-g91: questo monte = 'this mountain' (subject). salìo is an archaic past form meaning 'rose' or 'mounted' (past tense). verso ’l ciel tanto = 'so far/tall toward the sky/heaven' (verso 'toward', ’l ciel 'the sky/heaven', tanto 'so much/so far').
segments.purgatorio-28-g92: e libero n’è d’indi ove si serra: e = 'and'; libero + ne + è = 'is free of it/from that' (ne indicates separation/origin); d’indi = 'from there/thence'; ove si serra = 'where it is closed/enclosed' (ove = where, si serra = passive/reflexive 'is closed'). The clause says the mountain is free from the parts where it is enclosed.
segments.purgatorio-28-g93: Or perché = 'now because'. in circuito tutto quanto l’aere si volge = literal word order: 'in circuit' (in a circular motion), 'tutto quanto' = 'the whole/entire', l’aere = 'the air', si volge (reflexive) = 'turns itself/rotates', con la prima volta = 'with the first turning/revolution' (prima volta = first turn/time).
segments.purgatorio-28-g94: se non = 'unless' / 'if not'. li è rotto il cerchio = 'there is the circle broken' (li is locative/adverbial here), il cerchio d’alcun canto = 'the circle in any quarter/at any point' (d’alcun canto = in some corner/side). The clause means 'unless the circle is broken at some point.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g95: in questa altezza = 'at/in this elevation/height'. ch’è tutta disciolta = relative clause 'which is wholly dissolved/released' (disciolta = past participle used adjectivally). ne l’aere vivo = 'into the living air' (ne = in/into, l’aere vivo = the living/vivid air). The phrase locates the following effect at this high, airy region.
segments.purgatorio-28-g96: tal moto percuote: tal = 'such', moto = 'motion/impulse', percuote (3rd sing.) = 'strikes/strikes upon; shakes' — a concise clause saying 'such a motion strikes/affects.'
segments.purgatorio-28-g97: e fa sonar la selva = 'and makes the wood/forest resound' (fa + infinitive sonar = causative). perch’ è folta = perch' (short for perché) 'because' + è folta 'it is dense/thick' (referring to the wood). The clause links the motion to audible effects in the dense forest.
segments.purgatorio-28-g98: e la percossa pianta tanto puote: e = 'and'; la percossa pianta = 'the struck/affected plant/tree' (percossa = 'struck'); tanto modifies puote = 'so much/so greatly'; puote is an archaic form related to potere ('can/has power') meaning here 'is so powerful/has such potency'.
segments.purgatorio-28-g99: che de la sua virtute l’aura impregna = 'that the breeze/air impregnates it with its virtue' (che introduces result/cause): de la sua virtute = 'with/of its virtue/power'; l’aura = 'the breeze'; impregna (3rd sing.) = 'impregnates/soaks'. e quella poi = 'and that one (i.e. the tree) then...' linking to the following action.
segments.purgatorio-28-g100: girando = present participle/gerund 'turning/rotating' describing the subsequent motion of the impregnated thing (it turns as it acts).
segments.purgatorio-28-g101: intorno scuote = 'shakes all around' (intorno = 'around', scuote = 3rd sing. of scuotere 'shakes'). The gerund + this verb indicates that, as it turns, it shakes the surroundings.
segments.purgatorio-28-g102: e l’altra terra = 'and the other earth/soil' (the lower, different kind of earth as opposed to the airy region just described).
segments.purgatorio-28-g103: secondo ch’è degna = 'according as it is worthy/fitting' (secondo = 'according to', ch'è = 'which is', degna = 'worthy/fitting'). per sé e per suo ciel = 'by itself and by its heaven' — i.e. by its own nature and by the sky/celestial influence above it — specifying the grounds on which the earth acts.
segments.purgatorio-28-g104: concepe e figlia = verbs applied to the earth: concepe (conceives/gets pregnant) and figlia (gives birth) used figuratively to mean 'produces' or 'brings forth'. di diverse virtù diverse legna = 'from different virtues/forces (di diverse virtù) different timbers/woods (diverse legna)' — word order puts the source (virtues) before the produced items (legna).

sentences.purgatorio-28-s21: After hearing this, it would no longer seem a marvel there if any plant, without any visible seed, were to take root there.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s22: And to learn that the holy countryside where you are is full of every seed, and that it bears fruit within itself which there does not wither.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s23: The water you see does not spring from a vein that would restore vapors and turn them to frost, like a river that gains and loses breath; rather it issues from a firm and certain fountain, which so much takes back from the will of God as it pours forth, being open on two sides.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s24: From this side, with virtue, it descends that takes away from others the memory of sin; from the other, it restores to every good deed its due.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s9: No sooner had she reached the place where the grasses were already wetted by the waves of the fair river than she granted me the gift of lifting her eyes.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s10: I do not think that so bright a light shone under Venus's lashes, pierced by her son and contrary to all her custom.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s11: She laughed from the opposite bank, straight on, moving more colours with her hands than the high land throws up without seed.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s12: The river made us three paces apart; but the Hellespont, where Xerxes crossed, remained a restraint on all human prides, and it did not suffer more hatred from Leander for the storming between Sestos and Abydos than that one suffered from me because it did not open then.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s29: I turned myself then completely back to my poets, and I saw that, with a smile, they had heard the final utterance; then I turned my face toward the beautiful lady.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s5: With my feet I stopped and with my eyes I passed from there by the little stream to look at the great variety of fresh May-days; and there appeared to me, just as the sky suddenly shows a thing that by wonder diverts every other thought, a lone woman who was already going, and singing, and picking flower after flower, from which her whole path was painted.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s6: “Ah, fair lady, who are warmed by the rays of Love, if I may trust the looks that are wont to be witnesses of the heart, deign to will to draw yourself forward,” I said to her, “toward this riverbank, so that I may be able to understand that you are singing.”
sentences.purgatorio-28-s7: You make me remember where and what Proserpina was at the time when she lost her mother — and she was Spring.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s8: As she turned, with her soles pressed to the ground and inward to herself, like a woman who dances and scarcely puts foot before foot, she turned up the little vermilion and little yellow flowers toward me, no otherwise than a maiden who lowers her modest eyes; and she made my entreaties be satisfied, approaching so close that the sweet sound came to me with her intent.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s25: From this side is the Lethe; on the other side is called Eunoe, and it is of no use unless it has first been tasted both here and there: this one is above all other flavours.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s26: And although your thirst may be sufficiently satisfied because I no longer disclose it to you, I will, as a favour, give you one more corollary; nor do I think my telling will be less welcome to you if it goes beyond mere promise.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s27: Those who in ancient times sang of the Age of Gold and its happy condition perhaps dreamed of this place on Parnassus.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s28: Here the human root was innocent; here springtime always and every fruit; this is the nectar of which everyone speaks.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s13: “You are new, and perhaps because I laugh,” she began, “in this place chosen as a nest for human nature you feel some suspicion from wondering; but the Psalm ‘Delectasti’ gives light, which can dispel the clouding of your intellect.”
sentences.purgatorio-28-s14: And you, who are in front and who asked me, say if you wish to hear anything else; for I came ready for any question of yours, as much as will suffice.”
sentences.purgatorio-28-s15: “The water,” I said, “and the sound of the wood press within me a new faith concerning something I heard that is contrary to this.”
sentences.purgatorio-28-s16: Wherefore she: “I will tell how it comes about, by its own cause, that which makes you marvel, and I will cleanse the fog that smites you.”
sentences.purgatorio-28-s1: Already eager to search in and around the divine, thick, living forest — which the new day tempered to the eyes — without waiting any longer I left the bank, taking the countryside slowly, slowly up over the ground that on every side is full of olive trees.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s2: A sweet breeze, having no change in itself, brushed my brow with no more a blow than a gentle wind; by it the leaves, trembling, were all ready and bent toward the side where the first shade casts the holy mountain; not, however, so much scattered from their being upright that the little birds on the treetops would cease to practise all their art; but the first hours, with full joy, singing, received them inside the leaves — which kept time with his verses — just as, from branch to branch, it gathers through the pinewood up onto the strand of uproar, when Æolus, foolish, unlooses (the winds).
sentences.purgatorio-28-s3: By now my slow steps had carried me so far into the ancient wood that I could not see from where I had entered; and behold, a stream took me on farther, which, turning toward the left with its small waves, bent the grass that had risen on its bank.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s4: All the waters on this side that are more purified seemed to have some admixture of that one in themselves — which hides nothing — although it moves dark-brown under the perpetual shade that never allows sun or moon to shine there.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s17: The Highest Good, who delights only in himself, made man good and inclined to goodness, and gave this place to him as a step toward eternal peace.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s18: Because of his fault he stayed here only a little; because of his fault he changed honest laughter and sweet play into weeping and distress.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s19: Because the disturbance that the exhalations of the water and the earth make beneath them — which, as far as they can, follow the heat — would not make any war upon man, this mountain rose so high toward heaven, and is free from that region where it is closed.
sentences.purgatorio-28-s20: Now, because all the air in its circuit turns with the first revolution — if the circle is not broken at some point — in this height, which is wholly dissolved into the living air, such a motion strikes, and makes the forest resound because it is dense; and the struck tree is so powerful that the breeze soaks it with its virtue and then, turning, shakes it all around; and the other earth, according as it is fitting by itself and by its heaven, conceives and begets different timbers from different virtues.
